query: Did Dr. Bernhard Giesenhagen introduce or describe the bone ring technique in orthopedic surgery?

retrieved_docs.title: Ankle Arthroplasty -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: More modern third-generation implants have been designed to counter these causes of failure. [9] Examples of third-generation TAA implants include the Salto (Tornier SA, Saint Ismier, France), Hintegra (Newdeal SA, Lyon), Mobility (DePuy, Warsaw, IN), and Bologna-Oxford (BOX; Finsbury Orthopaedics, Leatherhead, Surrey, UK). These implants use a standalone polyethylene component not built into the tibial or talar components. They require minimal bone resection; however, they do require adequate balancing for implant success. These implants rely heavily on the integrity of the ligamentous structures surrounding the ankle. As implant technology has advanced, implant instrumentation has been refined, often incorporating cutting jigs. Preoperative computed tomography planning and patient-specific instrumentation in 3-dimensional printed cutting guides have evolved to reduce operative time and fluoroscopic exposure without compromising implant position. [10]

retrieved_docs.content: This article will attempt to outline the basic concepts of orthopedic biomaterials. This article will remain focused on the core materials used in orthopedics. This article will not discuss suture materials, biologics, graft materials, or implant coatings such as hydroxyapatite.

retrieved_docs.title: Wrist Arthrodesis -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Bone grafts are sometimes used during the procedure, depending on the surgeon's preference, experience, and patient requirements. The bone graft can either be harvested directly from the patient during the procedure or can be used from a tissue donation bank.

retrieved_docs.title: Arthroereisis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A few years later, Baker and Hill advocated a lateral opening-wedge osteotomy of the posterior joint surface to achieve the same goal. [4] Haraldsson was the first to coin the term "arthrohisis" to describe the insertion of a wedge graft into the sinus tarsi. [5] [6] In 1970, Lelièvre first used the term "arthroereisis" to describe a similar technique involving the insertion of a bone graft into the sinus tarsi, secured with a temporary staple. [7] The concept of supporting the talus on the calcaneus by inserting an external synthetic implant in the sinus tarsi quickly evolved. In 1974, Subotnick proposed the first device to achieve this, and since then, various solutions have been introduced, differing in form (block, sphere, screw, cap, cylinder) and composition (polyethylene, silastic, titanium, or a combination of these). [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.content: The following items are necessary for a standard uniplanar external fixation device using blunt pins. The size of the pin and drill will depend on the device and the bone where it will be in use. 15-blade scalpel Dissection scissors Soft tissue retractors Size-specific trocar with soft tissue protectant sleeve Corresponding drill bit T-handle wrench Tapping drill Corresponding size pins Bars Clamps C-arm fluoroscopy

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Nonunion -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Oligotrophic nonunion: Use a combination of both internal fixation and biologic stimulation depending on the clinical situation Infected nonunion: Must obtain WBC, ESR, CRP, and nuclear bone scan. Intraoperative cultures are the gold standard for guided antibiotic therapy A 2-staged surgical treatment protocol is the gold standard 1st stage - removal of loose or chronic infected hardware, debridement, and revision fixation of nonunion, and treatment of infection with culture-specific local and systemic antibiotics Modalities used for initial fixation in case of infection Antibiotic beads Antibiotic nails Antibiotic cement spacers Masquelet technique External fixation Soft tissue coverage with a flap 2nd stage Begins after a period of antibiotic therapy when both serologic and clinical signs of infection are absent Definitive fixation proceeds with internal fixation and bone grafting, other biological treatment, bone transport, depending on specific fracture characteristics.

retrieved_docs.content: Wilhelm Röentgen, in 1895 used the mathematical "X" to describe unknown rays, which he discovered, and two weeks after his discovery, he produced the first X-ray image of his wife’s hand, the first medical imaging photo published in December 1895. [1] The New York Times then predicted the “transformation of modern surgery by enabling the surgeon to detect the presence of foreign bodies.” Since his discovery, X-rays have advanced from fuzzy images of bones and foreign bodies to 3D cone beam imaging within a century since his discovery.

retrieved_docs.title: Ankle Arthroplasty -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Second-generation implants were developed to combat the large bone cuts required in first-generation, which were fixed bearing with a polyethylene bearing surface built into the talar or tibial components. Examples of second-generation implants included the Buechel-Pappas Total Ankle Replacement (Endotec, South Orange, NJ) in the United States and the Scandinavian Total Ankle Replacement (STAR; Waldemar Link, Hamburg, Germany) in Europe. This generation had more conservative bone cuts to allow for more bone preservation and were press fit. Like the first-generation models, however, there was a high failure rate due to increased polyethylene wear.

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Musculoskeletal Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Indications -- Periprosthetic joint infection
retrieved_docs.content: Implantation of arthroplasty hardware may cause an abnormal distribution of hematopoietic bone marrow, complicating the interpretation of results. Adding a 99mTc-Sulfur Colloid scan may offer better discrimination of normal bone marrow and reactive leukocyte infiltrates in infection. [25] Three-phase bone scintigraphy combined with labeled leukocyte/sulfur colloid marrow scintigraphy is the current gold standard nuclear medicine imaging for PJI. It offers high sensitivity, specificity, and diagnostic accuracy for PJI. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bones -- Clinical Significance -- Bone Fractures

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: External fixation is used to stabilized different bones across the body, but the overall technique of application remains the same. The pin-bone interface is critical for structural integrity. The first step includes incising the skin over the pin insertion site. Care is necessary that skin and muscle are not tenting on the pin because this may lead to inflammation and pin infections. [9] [10] Small Penfield-type retractors can help reflect the periosteum from the underlying bone. A trocar and drill sleeve is advanced to the bone to minimize entrapped tissue. The drill sleeve should be centered over the bone so that it traverses the near cortex, the medullary canal and exits the far cortex. Predrilling is best done with copious irrigation to prevent thermal necrosis of the bone interface. On the other hand, self-drilling pins have a drill tip point that the surgeon can place without pre-drilling. A biomechanical study by Awndrianne et al. demonstrated that self-drilling pins have 25% less purchase than non-drilling pins because the near cortex sometimes gets stripped by the resistance the screw encounters as it rotates before breaching at the far cortex. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture healing physiology largely depends on the mode of fixation and level of stability. With absolute fracture stability such as compression plating, the bone will undergo primary intramembranous bone healing. On the other hand, relative fracture stability, such as external fixation, results in secondary enchondral bone healing. There are also several ways to alter the external fixation construct to make the fracture more or less stable.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bone markings arise through a combination of genetic programming, mechanical stimuli, and adaptation to functional demands, resulting in a diverse array of features that serve various anatomical and physiological roles. [1] [2] Bone markings hold significant importance in surgery as they serve as crucial landmarks for surgical procedures. [3] Surgeons rely on bone markings to guide incisions, identify anatomical structures, and navigate around critical areas such as nerves and blood vessels. On the other hand, maladaptive bony prominences can impair normal anatomical function and contribute to musculoskeletal dysfunction and pain. Understanding bone markings enables clinicians to evaluate and manage various musculoskeletal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: The radius is identified as an allowed qualifier in several specialized domains, indicating that it can be the focus of detailed study or intervention in each area. In ultrastructural research, the radius is an allowed qualifier, meaning that subcellular imaging and analysis are applicable to this bone. Anatomical and histological investigations also list the radius as an allowed qualifier, reflecting its significance in the study of bone structure and tissue composition. Cytological assessment includes the radius as an allowed qualifier, allowing cellular‑level examination of bone cells. In the context of transplantation, the radius is an allowed qualifier, signifying that it may be considered for grafting or transplant procedures. Clinically, the radius is the direct_procedure_site_of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna, highlighting its central role in orthopedic care and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is highlighted as a versatile orthopedic technique that stabilizes bones without prior reduction and is applied not only to the femur and hip but also to other lower‑extremity bones, the radius and ulna, and the clavicle. Collectively, these entities illustrate the extensive array of surgical options—ranging from fixation, reduction, and growth arrest to osteoplastic remodeling, prosthetic revision, soft‑tissue repair, and prophylaxis—available for managing structural, functional, and developmental problems of the thigh and leg.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1197
retrieved_docs.content: Arthrodesis of elbow is classified as an “Other OR therapeutic procedure on joints,” indicating that it is a surgical intervention aimed at treating joint pathology. This procedure falls under the domain of Orthopaedic Surgery (physician group), reflecting that it is performed by orthopaedic surgeons who specialize in musculoskeletal disorders and joint reconstruction.

By connecting specific fracture entities, a comprehensive set of internal‑fixation and open‑reduction techniques, and hierarchical procedural categories to underlying bone‑density disorders, the knowledge base supports integrated treatment planning for orthopedic injuries of the hip, lower extremity, and shoulder‑arm region, while highlighting the importance of managing osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk and optimize surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: A wide range of orthopedic interventions are linked to the femur, reflecting the bone’s central role in lower‑extremity surgery. The procedure described as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” is associated with the femur, indicating that epiphyseal arrest techniques may involve the distal femur in conjunction with the proximal tibia and fibula. Direct surgical actions on the femur include “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” each of which specifies the femur as the direct procedure site. Procedures that are performed on or modify the femur more generally are identified as having the femur as the procedure site, such as “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Interventions that involve the femur in a less direct manner are classified with an indirect relationship, exemplified by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Suture of periosteum of femur,” both of which denote the femur as an indirect procedure site. Together, these relationships delineate how various surgical techniques—ranging from fracture fixation and bone shortening to prosthetic revision and soft‑tissue repair—are organized around the femur, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding femoral‑focused orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment modality for several specific orthopedic injuries. It is considered a type of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, for fractures or dislocations of the lower extremity excluding the hip and femur, and for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna. In each of these contexts, the procedure involves surgically stabilizing the bone without performing a reduction of the fracture, thereby providing a consistent therapeutic approach across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category is a comprehensive treatment modality for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur as well as for lower‑extremity injuries that do not involve the hip or femur.
